query: hepatitis B immune globulin and vaccine effectiveness

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of the hepatitis B vaccine. Identify the administration regimens of the hepatitis B vaccine. Evaluate the monitoring for patients receiving the hepatitis B vaccine, including any toxicity. Outline the importance of improving care coordination amongst the healthcare team to enhance care delivery for patients receiving the hepatitis B vaccine. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: This strategy has been largely effective. The vaccination itself benefits not just the vaccinated but the entire community. The increasing immunization rate has reduced morbidity and mortality from the virus. [1] From 2000 to 2014, the rate of acute hepatitis B infection in adults dropped by 50%. [1] This effect was mainly attributed to the herd immunity effect. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The first hepatitis B vaccine received FDA approval for use in the United States in 1981, with a recombinant version coming to market in 1986 that replaced the original blood-derived vaccine. [3] The immunization program for hepatitis B changed significantly in 1991 in the United States with a big push to eradicate the infection and substantially reduce its resultant morbidity and mortality. In 1991, the United States started a strategy to achieve universal vaccination of infants beginning at birth for hepatitis B. [2] The vaccine has numerous other indications. In addition to all infants and any yet unvaccinated children, the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices recommends primarily vaccinating any adults who may have a higher risk for contracting or complication from hepatitis B, including any of the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The hepatitis B vaccine is a medication used to prevent hepatitis B infection, which can lead to chronic liver failure and hepatocellular carcinoma. This activity reviews the indications, actions, and contraindications of the hepatitis B vaccine as a valuable agent in preventing hepatitis B infection. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other vital factors pertinent to healthcare team members in reducing the burden of hepatitis B on their patients and community.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive measures constitute a major component of the management of hepatitis B. As of 2019, hepatitis B vaccines available in the United States are categorized into either single-antigen hepatitis B vaccines or combination vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The interprofessional health care team faces many challenges when vaccinating patients for hepatitis B. While few medical interventions have as significant an impact on health as vaccinations, and the hepatitis B vaccination is generally safe, lack of knowledge about the vaccine by healthcare team members and patient concern about adverse events can decrease coverage. Challenges on knowledge of the vaccine for healthcare team members include staying current on evolving recommendations for whom the vaccine is indicated. [1] [12] [15] All team members can increase the vaccination rate of their patients by encouraging all staff to become trained in the assessment of vaccination histories and any pertinent staff in administering the vaccine. Protecting the time available for the team to do this is an essential component of this strategy. All patients should receive education on the benefits of vaccination, including its herd immunity effect, the generally safe side effect profile, and the relatively few contraindications. A presumption of acceptance may be effective with most patients. [1] [15] The hesitant parent and patient who does not respond to this can pose a challenge in vaccination. [15] Motivational interviewing techniques have shown to be effective with these hesitant patients. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hepatitis B Vaccination (HBV)
retrieved_docs.content: Catch-up vaccination: The 3-dose series can be started at any age for unvaccinated persons at 0, 1 to 2, or 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis B surface antibody titers increase quickly following vaccination and decrease over time. [1] Over 90% of immunized children develop protective antibody levels after the first dose. [12] [13] An immunocompetent person receiving the complete series is generally protected indefinitely. The Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices does not generally recommend titers after vaccination, except for some health care workers and immunocompromised persons. [1] In health care workers at risk for occupational exposure, serologic testing is recommended 1 to 2 months after the last vaccine dose, and revaccination with ≥one dose is recommended for non-responders. [14] [13] Healthcare workers who completed the vaccine series and had a subsequent positive titer (≥10 mIU/mL anti-HBs) are considered hepatitis B immune. They do not need any further routine testing for protection as long as they remain immune-competent. [14] All persons receiving the vaccine should remain under observation for at least 15 minutes following its administration for syncope or signs of anaphylaxis. [4] [12] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Intramuscular injection is the preferred administration method. The intradermal route is not recommended. The preferred site of injection is the deltoid muscle. The current preferred method is a regimen of 3 doses of the recombinant vaccine over 6 months. A new vaccine has received approval for adults in the United States, which requires just 2 doses in 1 month. [1] [4] After completing the 3-vaccination series, patients may receive a blood test after 1 to 4 months to determine the immune response, which is defined as an anti-hepatitis B surface antigen (anti-Hbs) antibody level exceeding 100mIU/mL. An antibody response between 10 and 100 mIU/mL demonstrates an inadequate response; these patients should get a single booster shot but do not require a retest. Responses below 10 mIU/mL require additional testing to check for prior hepatitis B infection, and these patients should receive another 3-dose regimen with testing again at the 1 to 4-month mark. Continued failed response warrants may require more aggressive interventions, including intradermal administration, high-dose vaccine, or double dosing of combination hepatitis A and B. Should this fail, the patient needs hepatitis B immunoglobulin upon exposure to the hepatitis B virus. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hepatitis B Vaccination (HBV)
retrieved_docs.content: If the neonate's mother has positive HBsAg, give the Hepatitis B vaccine and hepatitis B immune globulin within twelve hours. For infants under 2 Kg, an additional vaccine dose is needed at 1 month. At age 9 to 12 months, the infant should be tested for HBsAg and anti-HBs.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis A Vaccine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Community outbreaks of Hepatitis A have decreased since the implementation of recommended childhood HAV programs. In the event of a community-wide outbreak, accelerated vaccination can be a control measure. There is an increased risk of outbreaks occurring in people with higher risk (e.g., drug abusers or MSM) unless we achieve better vaccine coverage in these high-risk groups. Unfortunately, all programs initiated to contain hepatitis A outbreaks in these high risk have met with limited success until now. Efforts should instead focus on starting and continuing the routine vaccination of these groups. [4] In cases where an exposed person has not previously received the HAV, Immunoglobulins should be administered as early as possible after exposure to prevent infection with hepatitis A. In cases where a person has not completed their HAV series. However, if it has been >1 month since their initial dose of HAV, they do not need IG prophylaxis. The effectiveness of administering immunoglobulins more than two weeks after exposure to hepatitis A is not effective in preventing infection. Screening of contacts for immunity is not recommended before the administration of IG to avoid delay in postexposure treatment. It is advisable to confirm the IgM antibody testing in the index patient before giving initiating post-exposure therapy in the contacts. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B Vaccine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The hepatitis B vaccine is contraindicated in individuals with a history of hypersensitivity to any vaccine components or yeast hypersensitivity. [12] [11] Therefore, persons with a history of serious adverse events, such as anaphylaxis, after a previous hepatitis B vaccination (including combination vaccines) should not receive any further doses. The vaccine is not contraindicated in persons with a history of Guillain-Barre syndrome, autoimmune disease, current pregnancy, or lactation. The vaccine contains a noninfectious surface antigen, posing no risk of fetal transmission. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hepatitis B Vaccination (HBV)
retrieved_docs.content: Three dose series are recommended at 0, 1 to 2, and 6 to 18 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis A Vaccine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccination administration should be as close to the recommended intervals as possible. An interruption in the vaccination schedule does not require restarting the entire hepatitis A vaccine series or the addition of extra doses. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is organized hierarchically to include human vaccination, and human vaccination in turn encompasses التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Injection of RhD immune globulin represents a specific form of التمنيع السلبي, illustrating that this RhD immune‑globulin injection is an example of passive immunization used within the broader context of human vaccination strategies for autoimmune disease prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations represent a broad category of therapeutic interventions that encompass immunization, injections, and perfusion. Within this overarching group, human vaccination is a specific subclass, and it further includes distinct forms such as immunization for autoimmune disease and passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي). Passive immunization, in turn, comprises interventions like the injection of RhD immune globulin, which is recognized as a type of passive immunization. Additionally, prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified both as a therapeutic intervention (under immunization, injections, and perfusion) and as a component of the larger prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations category. This hierarchical organization highlights how various vaccination and immunization strategies are interrelated, ranging from general preventive measures to specialized applications for autoimmune conditions and passive immune protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). This specific immunoprophylactic intervention provides immediate, short‑term protection by supplying pre‑formed antibodies, thereby preventing Rh sensitization in Rh‑negative patients exposed to Rh‑positive blood. As a passive immunization strategy, the injection of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how targeted antibody administration can be used to avert alloimmunization and its associated complications in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is administered through the injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and is classified as a child procedure within the broader category of such therapeutic injections or infusions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a specific child category, indicating that human vaccination is a form of immunization directed toward autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is also placed under the broader class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role in preventive health measures. Within human vaccination, there is a subcategory called التمنيع السلبي, which represents a particular type of negative immunization. Injection of RhD immune globulin is linked to التمنيع السلبي through an inverse hierarchical relationship, suggesting that this injection is a specific instance encompassed by the broader concept of التمنيع السلبي. The category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations classifies itself, emphasizing its function as a primary classification for preventive immunization strategies. Together, these relationships illustrate a structured taxonomy of vaccination and immunization practices, from general preventive categories to specific interventions such as RhD immune globulin, aiding comprehensive understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations are identified as a category that classifies itself, meaning the concept of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations is used to define and encompass the same set of preventive immunizations and inoculation practices. This self‑referential classification underscores that the term represents a distinct group of preventive interventions within medical terminology.
